lines.text: Section two. Genome sequences.
If you had to name one single technological event that made synthetic biology possible, the sequencing of complete genomes would be the strongest candidate.
The Human Genome Project, declared essentially complete in 2003, is the most famous example.
But for synthetic biology, the more pivotal events came a few years earlier.
In 1996, the genome of the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae was published.
In 1997, the genome of Escherichia coli was published.

Both organisms are workhorses of biotechnology and the most heavily used chassis cells in modern synthetic biology, and having their complete genomes finally available transformed the kind of work that could be done.
Why is having a genome sequence so important?
Because a genome sequence is, in a literal sense, the digital blueprint of the cell.
Once you have the full DNA sequence in hand, you can systematically annotate every gene, every promoter, every regulatory region, every metabolic pathway.
You can ask which genes are essential and which are redundant.
You can identify all the parts that could potentially be used in engineering, and you can identify all the cellular machinery that engineered parts will have to coexist with.
Before genome sequences, synthetic biology had to work with fragments — a few characterised genes here, a known pathway there.
With complete genomes, the field has a global view of its chassis cells.
After E. coli and yeast, the floodgates opened.
Many more microbial genomes were sequenced.
Plant genomes followed — Arabidopsis thaliana, the model plant.
Metazoan animal genomes followed — Drosophila melanogaster the fruit fly, Caenorhabditis elegans the worm.
And eventually the human genome, several primate genomes, and now thousands of others.

Each new sequenced organism adds to the gene toolbox available to synthetic biologists — promoters and proteins and pathways from any organism in nature can, in principle, be ported into a chassis cell, given the appropriate engineering work.
The authors describe these genome sequences as the basis for "parts" in synthetic biology, and that framing is exactly right.
Every promoter, every coding sequence, every terminator in the modern synthetic biology registry can be traced back to some genome from which it was first read.
Without genome sequences, there would be nothing to read and nothing to copy.
The whole edifice of parts-based biological engineering rests on the digital availability of biological sequence data.
